payload.text:
Create a premium, elegant personal resume blog website for me. The entire website should be in a sleek dark theme with high contrast and smooth UI transitions. Use modern SAP-inspired design principles and SAP Fiori/SAP 72 font style consistently across the site.

The website should serve as my personal portfolio + resume, covering everything from my school education to my latest professional experience as an SAP ABAP & SAP Developer. Include dedicated sections for:

• About Me
• Education Timeline (School → College → Certifications)
• Professional Journey (Internships → Full-time roles → Current role)
• Skills (ABAP, SAP RAP, Fiori, OData, CDS, BTP, etc.)
• Projects (SAP development, automation, AI integrations, RAP apps, etc.)
• Achievements & Awards
• Blog/Articles section where I write about SAP technologies
• Contact section with email & social links

Design Requirements:
• Use a dark premium UI with elegant gradients.
• Use SAP Fiori–inspired spacing, iconography, and typography (SAP 72 font).
• Smooth animations (fade-ins, motion on scroll).
• Rounded corners, glass-morphism or neo-brutalism accents.
• Professional, minimalistic yet luxurious look.
• Must be fully responsive on mobile, tablet, and desktop.

Technical Requirements:
• Clean semantic HTML/CSS/JS (or React if applicable).
• Modular code structure.
• SEO-friendly & fast loading.
• Include a CMS-like structure for blog posts.

Overall goal: Deliver a premium dark portfolio website that showcases my entire journey as an SAP developer in a visually compelling and professional way.

**Considering the Scope**

I'm currently focused on defining the project's parameters. The user's vision is crystal clear: a dark, Fiori-inspired website highlighting their SAP expertise. I'm prioritizing the structure, key sections (About, Skills, Experience, Projects), and the desired aesthetic. The "SAP 72" font style is a critical detail I'm making sure to integrate, along with a sleek, professional layout that mirrors high-end Fiori applications. This approach will maximize my attention to detail.


**Refining Design Elements**

I'm now fully immersed in the design phase. I've resolved the font issue by opting for a system font stack (San Francisco/Inter/Roboto) or Google Fonts (Inter/Roboto), configured to closely resemble the "72" font. The color palette will be dark and premium, incorporating deep blacks, dark greys and SAP-like accent colors within a dark mode aesthetic. I'm leaning toward a card-based layout with a timeline for experience and a grid for skills to stay Fiori-inspired.


**Mapping Out Components**

I've outlined the core component structure. I'm focusing on `Layout.tsx` and planning the overall flow. I'll need to define the type and mock data. For styling, I'll leverage Tailwind, configuring the color palette for a dark theme and "72" font resemblance by using `font-sans` with `Inter` loaded from Google Fonts.  The `Hero` section's visual appeal, using large text and a gradient background, is a high priority.



**Developing Page Structure**

I'm now focusing on discrete sections. I'm dividing the `Experience` section into distinct "Education" and "Professional" timelines, organizing the `Skills` section with categorized chips or progress bars for clarity. The `Blog` will feature a grid of cards, and `Contact` will be a streamlined form with social links. I'm also addressing responsiveness with grid column adjustments based on screen width.


**Adjusting for Technicalities**

I've resolved the font issue by selecting a close alternative and configured the CSS `font-family` property. I've also reconsidered the page structure to align better with the user's intent. Initially, I thought a single page was ideal, but a blog feature requires more complexity. I've decided on a single-page application with smooth scrolling to maintain a straightforward user experience.


**Implementing Transitions**

I'm now implementing the core React components. I've decided against `framer-motion`, opting for Tailwind CSS transitions and an IntersectionObserver hook for scroll reveal effects, given the project's constraints. I'm leveraging `lucide-react` for icons and I've established the component structure, including `metadata.json`, `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, `types.ts`, and `constants.ts` to store resume content. I'm also building out core components: `Navbar`, `Hero`, `Section`, `Experience`, `Skills`, `Projects`, `Blog`, and `Contact`.


**Choosing Color Scheme**

I've finalized the color palette for the dark mode Fiori aesthetic. The background will be a deep charcoal, specifically either `#181818` or `#0f0f0f`, while the card backgrounds will be a slightly lighter `#252525`. For accents, I'm leaning towards a lighter blue, `#5899DA`, reminiscent of SAP blue, along with a touch of gold, `#F0AB00`. I'll set the font family to `'Inter', sans-serif` in my Tailwind configuration for a modern feel.

2.  **Add `vite.config.ts`**: Configures the Vite build tool to use the React plugin. This ensures that your `.tsx` files are correctly transpiled into browser-ready JavaScript during deployment.
3.  **Add `tsconfig.json`**: Provides the TypeScript compiler with instructions on how to handle your code, ensuring compatibility with modern React features and Vite's bundling logic.
4.  **Update `index.html`**: Adds an explicit `<script type="module" src="/index.tsx"></script>` tag. While some environments handle this automatically, a standard Vite build (required for Vercel) needs this tag to identify the entry point of your application.

    "noEmit": true,
    "jsx": "react-jsx"
  },
  "include": ["**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
